data.title: What's Behind The Drop In Trio-Tech International Stock?
data.description:
The pullback is playing out even as the Nasdaq is leading the market higher today, with traders also watching how fast momentum names can unwind after Trio-Tech was recently up 41% to $11.72 in a recent high-beta tape.

  • Trio-Tech Intl stock is feeling bearish pressure. What’s behind TRT decline?

Trio-Tech To Raise $10 Million For AI Investments

Trio-Tech announced the pricing of a $10 million registered direct offering, with D. Boral Capital LLC acting as the exclusive placement agent. The company says it plans to use net proceeds for working capital and general corporate purposes, including strategic investments tied to AI and automotive growth opportunities.

The broader market is leaning positive, with the S&P 500 up 0.44% and the Nasdaq up 1.40% as Technology gains 2.38%. Trio-Tech's drop is happening while those benchmarks are rising, pointing to a stock-specific reaction to the financing news.

Shares Stay Above Key Averages

Trio-Tech is still holding a stretched uptrend on longer-term measures, but Friday's selloff fits the profile of a momentum stock that can snap back sharply when new supply hits the tape. The stock is trading 83% above its 20-day simple moving average (SMA) and 112.7% above its 100-day SMA, which signals strong trend control by buyers even after the pullback.

The relative strength index (RSI), a momentum gauge, is 87.98, which is deep in overbought territory and often lines up with "air pocket" risk if sentiment shifts. RSI at 87.98 means the stock has been rising so fast that even small negatives can trigger fast profit-taking.

  • Key Resistance: $16.50 — near the prior peak area where rallies have recently stalled.
  • Key Support: $7.50 — closer to the rising short-term trend zone where dip-buyers have tended to show up.

Trio-Tech Leans Into AI Infrastructure

Trio-Tech International is a provider of reliability test equipment and services to the semiconductor and other industries, with customers spanning automotive electronics, industrial electronics, computing and data storage, consumer electronics, and communications.

It operates across Semiconductor Back-end Solutions and Industrial Electronics, with a footprint in the U.S., Singapore, Malaysia, Thailand and China. That mix matters today because the company is explicitly linking the offering proceeds to capacity expansion and growth opportunities tied to AI and automotive end markets.

Mega-cap earnings-driven risk appetite provides a benchmark for Trio-Tech because sharp moves in bellwethers often set the tone for small-cap momentum and financing-sensitive names. Tesla's quarter showed revenue of $22.71 billion (up 16% year over year) and adjusted EPS of $0.41, but the stock still fell over 1%, underscoring how quickly traders can fade "good news" when positioning is crowded.

Shares Slip On Friday

TRT Stock Price Activity: Trio-Tech Intl shares were down 9.22% at $12.51 at the time of publication on Friday, according to Benzinga Pro data.

Image: Shutterstock

data.title: Cable Giant Charter Stock Sinks After Customer Exodus Accelerates
data.description:
Details

The broadband and cable provider posted quarterly revenue of $13.597 billion, down 1.0% year over year but slightly above the analyst consensus estimate of $13.539 billion. Earnings came in at $9.17 per share, missing expectations of $9.98.

The revenue decline was driven by weaker residential video performance. Charter lost 120,000 internet customers in the quarter, compared with a decline of 59,000 a year earlier.

Total video customers fell by 60,000, an improvement from the loss of 181,000 in the prior-year period, aided by simplified pricing, packaging changes and the inclusion of streaming apps in Spectrum's expanded basic packages.

Mobile continued to be a bright spot. The company added 368,000 mobile lines, bringing the total to 12.1 million as of March 31, 2026.

Adjusted EBITDA margin narrowed 50 basis points to 41.5%.

Cash Flow and Spending

Free cash flow declined to $1.37 billion from $1.56 billion a year earlier, reflecting higher capital expenditures. Net cash provided by operating activities totaled $4.3 billion.

Charter ended the quarter with $517 million in cash and equivalents and served 29.6 million internet customers.

Charter Communications Earnings Call Highlights

Chief Executive Officer Chris Winfrey said Spectrum Mobile remains the fastest-growing provider in Charter's footprint, surpassing 12 million lines after adding about 370,000 during the quarter.

He also pointed to improving video trends, with customer losses narrowing, and said network upgrades should enable symmetrical, multi-gig speeds across roughly half the footprint by year-end.

Chief Financial Officer Jessica Fischer said revenue declined 1% primarily due to lower residential video revenue, while residential connectivity revenue rose 0.9%. Adjusted EBITDA fell 2.2%, or 1.8% excluding Cox-related transition costs.

Fischer said Charter continues to expect about $11.4 billion in capital expenditures in 2026 but anticipates annual capex falling below $8 billion after major network investments are completed.

Executives said the pending acquisition of Cox Communications is expected to expand Charter's footprint and generate at least $800 million in run-rate operating expense synergies, with potential for further upside.

Charter repurchased $963 million of stock during the quarter.

Outlook

The company reiterated its 2026 capital spending forecast of about $11.4 billion, compared with $11.7 billion in fiscal 2025.

Charter Communications Stock Tanks

CHTR Price Action: Charter Communications shares were down 22.57% at $187.20 at the time of publication on Friday. The stock is near its 52-week low of $180.38, according to Benzinga Pro data.

Photo via Shutterstock

data.title: If You Invested $1000 In Howmet Aerospace Stock 5 Years Ago, You Would Have This Much Today
data.description:
Howmet Aerospace (NYSE:HWM) has outperformed the market over the past 5 years by 38.33% on an annualized basis producing an average annual return of 49.58%. Currently, Howmet Aerospace has a market capitalization of $99.49 billion.

Buying $1000 In HWM: If an investor had bought $1000 of HWM stock 5 years ago, it would be worth $7,443.20 today based on a price of $248.15 for HWM at the time of writing.

Howmet Aerospace's Performance Over Last 5 Years

Finally -- what's the point of all this? The key insight to take from this article is to note how much of a difference compounded returns can make in your cash growth over a period of time.

This article was generated by Benzinga's automated content engine and reviewed by an editor.

data.title: Full Transcript: Principal Financial Group Q1 2026 Earnings Call
data.description:
On Friday, Principal Financial Group (NASDAQ:PFG) discussed first-quarter financial results during its earnings call. The full transcript is provided below.

This transcript is brought to you by Benzinga APIs. For real-time access to our entire catalog, please visit https://www.benzinga.com/apis/ for a consultation.

View the webcast at https://edge.media-server.com/mmc/p/ar5948e9/

Summary

Principal Financial Group reported a 13% growth in adjusted non-GAAP earnings per share for Q1 2026, driven by favorable underwriting results and improved mortality within benefits and protection, alongside positive market conditions for fee-based businesses.

The company returned approximately $375 million to shareholders, including $200 million in share repurchases, and raised its common stock dividend by 8% for the 12th consecutive quarter.

Strong momentum was seen in strategic growth areas such as the retirement ecosystem and global asset management, with significant increases in retirement transfer deposits and private markets AUM.

The company's capital position remains robust with over $1.4 billion in excess and available capital, and non-GAAP operating ROE improved to 16.1%.

Operational highlights include record gross sales in investment management, strong international pension performance, and favorable underwriting in specialty benefits.

Full Transcript

OPERATOR

Humphrey Lee (Vice President of Investor Relations)

Deanna Schrabel (Chief Executive Officer)

Joel Pitts (Chief Financial Officer)

OPERATOR

at this time. I would like to remind everyone that to ask a question, press star 11 on your telephone. We'll pause for just a moment to compile the Q and A roster. The first question comes from Ryan Krueger from kbw.

Ryan Krueger (Equity Analyst)

Hey, thanks. Good morning. My first question was on specialty benefits. Can you provide some more color on the favorable underwriting experience you had across dental life and disability and also just how you're thinking about the outlook from here.

Deanna Schrabel (Chief Executive Officer)

Yeah, Ryan, good morning and welcome back. Obviously it was a really strong quarter for specialty benefits and I'll pass it over to Amy to talk about the drivers.

Amy

OPERATOR

Ryan, do you have a follow up question?

Ryan Krueger (Equity Analyst)

Yeah. On investment management, you've seen this good momentum in gross sales, but then redemptions have also largely ticked up into the flows, haven't improved as much. So I was just hoping to get a little more color on, I mean maybe both sides of it, what's driving the growth sales momentum, but also why have you been seeing higher redemptions and how do you think that may play out from here?

Kamal

Ryan Krueger (Equity Analyst)

Thanks a lot.

Deanna Schrabel (Chief Executive Officer)

Yeah, thanks Ryan.

OPERATOR

Next question. The next question comes from Wes Carmichael from Wells Fargo.

Wes Carmichael (Equity Analyst)

Hey, good morning. Thank you. First question was on the individual life segment. I think just looking at results. I think it's the best quarter that that segment's produced in a long time and I typically think about the first quarter as being seasonally weak from a mortality perspective. So just wondering if you think anything in the earnings power has changed for that segment or is this just a little bit more one time ish in nature?

Deanna Schrabel (Chief Executive Officer)

Yeah, thanks west for this question for that question. Obviously life did have a very strong earnings quarter really driven by mortality. So I'll ask Amy to give some color around that.

Amy

Wes Carmichael (Equity Analyst)

Wes, do you have a follow up? Yeah, I do. Thank you. Thanks so much. And so just switching to ris, pretty strong transfer deposits. Just curious if you could maybe just touch on the flow outlook for that segment for the rest of the year.

Deanna Schrabel (Chief Executive Officer)

Yeah, I'LL turn it over to Chris. As you know we focus on revenue growth and ultimately really drove strong we did have very strong fundamentals across ris. Large case can tend to be lumpy when you look at transfer deposits and this was a quarter we benefited from that. But I'll ask Chris to give some more color.

Chris

Deanna Schrabel (Chief Executive Officer)

Thanks Chris and thanks Wes. Next question.

OPERATOR

The next question comes from Sunit Kamath from Jeffries.

Sunit Kamath (Equity Analyst)

Thanks. Morning. I wanted to start with RIS also on the advice model that you guys have and correct me if I'm wrong, but my understanding is that you use more of a call center model as opposed to sort of feet on the street or building out wealth management offices and I know one of your competitors is taking that latter approach. Just wondering if that's something that you guys have looked at or if it's something that you might consider.

Deanna Schrabel (Chief Executive Officer)

Yeah, thanks Sunit. Thanks for being there and for the question. As we've talked about, our focus is really on the majority of our participants and want to be able to broaden provide broad based support to those that don't have as much access or to the advisor community. And so I think our approach is different but I'll ask Chris to continue to give a little bit more insight into that.

Chris

Sunit Kamath (Equity Analyst)

Deanna Schrabel (Chief Executive Officer)

Amy

Deanna Schrabel (Chief Executive Officer)

Okay, thanks. Thanks Suneet for the questions.

OPERATOR

Next question.

Jack Matten

The next question comes from Jack Matten from BMO Capital Markets. Hey, good morning. My first one was on international pension. The earnings run rate took a nice step up this quarter, even kind of backing out the significant variances that you call out, I guess. Can you unpack some of the drivers there? And which do you think are kind of more repeatable or sustainable versus some of the more transitory factors like FX or elevated performance fees?

Deanna Schrabel (Chief Executive Officer)

Yeah, I'll ask Joel to talk through that. And again, thanks Jack for being here and for your questions. Obviously it was a strong earnings growth for international pension and that segment continues to provide some great diversification to our overall results and really focused on where we feel that we can drive growth. So I'll ask Joel to give some specifics on the quarter.

Joel Pitts (Chief Financial Officer)

Jack Matten

I hope that helps. And do you have a follow up question? Yes, thank you. Maybe just one. On the kind of the outlook for for VII and performance fees in the investment management business this year, do you have any visibility at this point to kind of the cadence of realizations and I guess to what extent do you think market conditions need to change or improve in order to kind of unlock a more normal level of real estate monetization?

Joel Pitts (Chief Financial Officer)

OPERATOR

Next question.

Wilma Burtis (Equity Analyst)

The next question comes from Wilma Burtis, from Raymond James. Hey, good morning. What drove the lower PRT sales in the quarter and is there a little bit more competition flowing into the SMB PRT market?

Deanna Schrabel (Chief Executive Officer)

Yeah, thanks Wilma, for the question. I'll ask Chris to address that.

Chris

Deanna Schrabel (Chief Executive Officer)

Yeah, well, man, and I think as we've discussed, we're not going to taste sales for the sake of sales. We're going to make sure we're disciplined on the capital we deploy and that we can get from that. And if it is lower, we're looking for other opportunities to ensure that we're driving profitable growth across the enterprise. So thanks for that question. Do you have a follow up?

Wilma Burtis (Equity Analyst)

Yes. Are you seeing any competition actually improving or decreasing in group dental? Given you guys have implemented price increases but you're still seeing healthy sales growth.

Amy

Wilma Burtis (Equity Analyst)

Thank you for the questions that did. Thank you.

OPERATOR

Next question. The next question comes from Tom Gallagher from Evercore ISI.

Tom Gallagher (Equity Analyst)

Good morning. First question, just on RAS fee flows. 1q25 I think you had a jumbo case that you lost. How were the jumbo case callouts for this quarter? Did you have any wins, losses? How did that influence RIS fee flows this quarter?

Deanna Schrabel (Chief Executive Officer)

I'll have Chris address that.

Chris

Tom Gallagher (Equity Analyst)

Thanks, Tom. Do you have a follow up?

Deanna Schrabel (Chief Executive Officer)

Yeah, Deanna. So my follow up I guess is for Kamal on performance. It looks like your one year numbers for equities and asset allocation got better. Fixed income slipped a little bit. Three year numbers fell across the board though in all three categories. Are you seeing any impact from the performance issues and why do you think the performance has slipped a bit here?

Kamal

Deanna Schrabel (Chief Executive Officer)

Thanks Tom, for the questions.

OPERATOR

Next question.

Michael Ward (Equity Analyst)

The next question comes from Michael Ward from ubs. Hi, thanks. Good morning. I was wondering, on the specialty benefits, did the MA that you did in the quarter, like did that contribute at all to new business growth and then are there other targets out there that you guys could look to transact on?

Deanna Schrabel (Chief Executive Officer)

Yeah, thanks Mike for that question. We did do a small dental network acquisition with a company that we had

Amy

Michael Ward (Equity Analyst)

Thanks, Mark. Do you have a follow up question? Yes, on ris. I guess you guys, I would say have been a little bit quieter just in terms of the inclusion of privates for retirement funds in retirement funds. I just curious your sort of stance on that issue and how you see that heading going forward for the industry.

Deanna Schrabel (Chief Executive Officer)

Yeah, I'll have Chris talk through that. Obviously we applaud and support thoughtful efforts to expand investment options within retirement plans. The recent DOL guidance is an important step, but I think our stance is going to take time, it's going to be slow and ultimately, as we talk to our plant, our customers, they're intrigued but are not pushing to move at a fast rate for inclusion. But I'll see if Chris has some additional flavor.

Chris

Deanna Schrabel (Chief Executive Officer)

Thanks Mike for those questions.

Michael Ward (Equity Analyst)

Thank you.

OPERATOR

Next question.

Pablo Singhon (Equity Analyst)

Our final question comes from Pablo Singhon from JP Morgan. Hi, good morning. So just to start off, maybe a question for ris. Wanted to ask about your efforts or the growth spread earnings, whether from as usual flows or am sitting in retiring plants. How do you see the fee versus spread mix evolving over time?

Deanna Schrabel (Chief Executive Officer)

Chris

Deanna Schrabel (Chief Executive Officer)

Thanks, Pablo. Hope that helps. Do you have a follow up?

Kamal

Thanks, Leanna. And secondly, maybe Kamal, I was hoping you could elaborate on your comment about the asset management pipeline being very strong. Is it better than it was a year ago or are you seeing new opportunities? Anything you can comment on there? Thanks.

Deanna Schrabel (Chief Executive Officer)

Yeah, that's a great final question. We do have a strong pipeline as we sit here today. I think volatility in the market could impact the timing of when that flows in. But I'll have Kamal give some additional color.

Kamal

Deanna Schrabel (Chief Executive Officer)

Thank you, Pablo. I hope that helps.

Pablo Singhon (Equity Analyst)

Thank you.

OPERATOR

We have reached the end of our Q&A. Ms. Drabel, your closing comments please.

Deanna Schrabel (Chief Executive Officer)

data.title: What's Behind The Drop In Ondas Stock Friday?
data.description:
Ondas Inc. (NASDAQ:ONDS) shares are trading lower on Friday, as the company pushed deeper into defense contracting through a major acquisition.

  • Ondas stock is under selling pressure. Why is ONDS stock trading lower?

Ondas Completes $175 Million Merger With Mistral

Ondas completed its merger with U.S. defense prime contractor Mistral in a deal valued at $175 million, adding programs in excess of $1 billion and expanding direct prime participation across U.S. Department of War programs. The company said the transaction adds about $264 million to backlog, with pro forma backlog (adjusted for the Mistral and World View acquisitions) at $457 million as of March 31, 2026.

The broader market is trading higher today, led by Technology up 2.17% and supported by a positive advance/decline ratio of 1.2. Communication Services is down 1.61% and currently ranks 11 out of 11 sectors, and Ondas is slipping a bit more than the group, pointing to stock-specific digestion on top of sector weakness.

Stocks Trades Above Key Averages

Ondas is still holding a strong longer-term uptrend position within its 52-week range, even as the stock works through a choppier stretch after its March swing high and swing low. The stock is trading 7.5% above its 20-day simple moving average (SMA) and 1.8% above its 100-day SMA, which leans bullish for trend support even if near-term swings stay active.

The moving average stack is mixed: the 20-day SMA is below the 50-day SMA (a short-term bearish alignment), but the golden cross that occurred in July 2025 keeps the longer-term backdrop constructive. The moving average convergence divergence (MACD), a trend/momentum measure, is above its signal line with a positive histogram, which points to improving upside momentum compared with the June 2025 bearish cross.

The 12-month gain of 1196.06% shows how powerful the longer-term move has been, which can also make pullbacks sharper as traders reassess expectations. With the 52-week low at 69 cents and the 52-week high at $15.28, the stock is sitting much closer to the upper end of its yearly range, so reactions around prior breakout zones can matter more.

  • Key Resistance: $12.00 — a level where rallies have recently had trouble pushing through.
  • Key Support: $8.50 — an area where buyers have tended to show up on pullbacks.

Earnings & Analyst Outlook

Looking further out, the next major catalyst for the stock arrives with the May 14, 2026 (estimated) earnings report.

  • EPS Estimate: Loss of 3 cents (Up from Loss of 15 cents YoY)
  • Revenue Estimate: $39.40 million (Up from $4.25 million YoY)
  • Valuation: P/E ratio not available

Analyst Consensus & Recent Actions: The stock carries a Buy rating with an average price target of $19.83. Recent analyst moves include:

  • Northland Capital Markets: Outperform (Raises Target to $18.00) (March 26)
  • Needham: Buy (Maintains Target to $23.00) (March 25)
  • Needham: Buy (Raises Target to $23.00) (March 24)

Shares Slip On Friday

ONDS Stock Price Activity: Ondas shares were down 0.95% at $10.44 at the time of publication on Friday, according to Benzinga Pro data.

Image: Shutterstock
